sections.heading: Surgery
sections.content:
Surgery is the primary local control modality for most resectable bone sarcomas and is essential for cure. It is used neoadjuvantly, adjuvantly, or as definitive therapy depending on tumor type, response to systemic therapy, and resectability.

Principles:
- Prior to biopsy, consultation with an orthopedic oncologist regarding appropriate prebiopsy imaging is critical [BONE-A].
- Biopsy is optimally performed at a center that will provide definitive management. Placement of biopsy is critical to subsequent limb-sparing surgery [BONE-A].
- Wide excision should achieve histologically negative surgical margins. Negative margins optimize local tumor control [BONE-A, BONE-C 3 of 6].
- Local tumor control may be achieved by either limb-sparing excision or limb amputation. Limb-sparing excision is preferred to optimize function if reasonable functional expectations can be achieved [BONE-A].
- Final pathologic evaluation should include assessment of surgical margins, size/dimensions of tumor, and response to preoperative therapy [BONE-A].
- Fresh tissue may be needed for biomarker testing and tissue banking [BONE-A].
- For specific tumor types: Intralesional excision may be adequate for atypical cartilaginous tumors of bone (low-grade). Wide excision is standard for high-grade chondrosarcoma, chordoma, osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma, and GCTB in select cases.

Resectability criteria: Resectability is determined by the multidisciplinary team based on tumor size, location, involvement of neurovascular structures, and expected functional outcome. For example, chordomas of the skull base are often considered resectable via intralesional excision with maximal safe removal, while those of the sacrum may require wide excision. For GCTB, resectability also considers the morbidity of the procedure.

Pre-operative optimisation: Optimization of patient performance status, nutritional status, and management of comorbidities. Consider preoperative embolization for hypervascular tumors (e.g., GCTB of sacrum). For Ewing sarcoma and osteosarcoma, neoadjuvant chemotherapy is standard to downstage tumor. Fertility preservation should be discussed prior to chemotherapy.

cards.name: Procedure
cards.detail: {"name":"Wide Excision","approach":"Resection of the tumor with a cuff of normal tissue to achieve negative margins. May be limb-sparing or amputation.","indications":"Standard for high-grade chondrosarcoma, chordoma, osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma, and GCTB (for aggressive stage III or unresectable axial lesions).","key_complications":"Wound complications, infection, neurovascular injury, functional loss, need for reconstruction.","operative_mortality":"Low, but varies with patient comorbidities and extent of surgery."}

cards.name: Procedure
cards.detail: {"name":"Intralesional Excision (Curettage)","approach":"Surgical removal of tumor contents from within its cavity, often with adjuvants (e.g., cryosurgery, phenol, cement).","indications":"Atypical cartilaginous tumors (low-grade intracompartmental appendicular tumors). May be considered for GCTB (especially resectable with acceptable morbidity).","key_complications":"Higher local recurrence rate compared to wide excision, especially for GCTB.","operative_mortality":"Low."}

cards.name: Procedure
cards.detail: {"name":"Amputation","approach":"Removal of the entire limb or part of it.","indications":"Selected cases of osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma, and other sarcomas where limb-sparing surgery cannot achieve negative margins or would result in a non-functional limb. Also for management of local relapse.","key_complications":"Prosthetic complications, phantom limb pain, psychological impact.","operative_mortality":"Low."}
